planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Gold Mound is evergreen and root-hardy in zone 9a with freeze protection; it may die back to the ground in hard freezes but recovers quickly from the roots in spring. Place in full sun for the most intense golden-yellow foliage — plants in shade develop a greenish tint and lose the characteristic gold coloration.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Gold Mound Duranta is a compact, golden-foliaged cultivar of the tropical Duranta that produces stunning chartreuse-to-golden-yellow leaves throughout the growing season, in dramatic contrast to the deep green foliage of surrounding plants. Unlike the standard blue-flowered Duranta erecta, Gold Mound rarely flowers or fruits, redirecting its energy into the vivid foliage display that makes it a premier landscape accent in zone 9–11. The compact, mounding habit (2–4 feet tall and wide) makes it far more manageable than the standard species, which can reach 15–20 feet without regular pruning. Gold Mound performs exceptionally well as a low border plant, foundation shrub, or container specimen in the Gulf Coast, where it provides year-round color in the landscape. The bright gold leaves intensify through summer heat, and the plant withstands the full force of Gulf Coast summers without wilting or losing color.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Duranta Cultivars for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Gold Mound Duranta for Texas Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Shrubs for Louisiana Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant March–April for spring and August for fall. Long of Naples produces very long vines (12–15 feet) requiring generous space or a large trellis. The moschata type is more heat-tolerant and powdery mildew-resistant than pepo squashes, making it a better choice for zone 9 summer production. Harvest immature fruits at 12–18 inches for summer squash use, or leave to mature fully for winter storage.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Long of Naples (Lunga di Napoli, Serpentina di Napoli) is a renowned Italian heirloom squash from the Campania region surrounding Naples, producing extraordinarily long cylindrical fruits of 24–36 inches or more — occasionally reaching 4–5 feet under optimal conditions. At maturity the tan-to-buff skin covers dense, deep-orange flesh with a sweet, rich flavor similar to butternut but with a creamier texture and less watery flesh. Immature fruits at 12–18 inches can be harvested and used as a superior summer squash — far more flavorful than standard zucchini, with firmer flesh and more concentrated sweetness. Fully mature fruits cure well and store for 6–12 months in a cool, dry location. As a Cucurbita moschata type, Long of Naples shows excellent heat and humidity tolerance, resistance to vine borers, and superior performance in zone 9 compared to pepo-type squashes. The vines are vigorous and spreading — allow 8–12 feet per plant.
sources: University of Naples Federico II — Campanian Heirloom Vegetable Varieties
Slow Food Foundation — Presidia Long of Naples Squash
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Moschata Squash Production

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplant date; in zone 9 transplant September–October for fall and January–February for spring. Grand Duke is an All-America Selections winner that performs best in cool weather — fall crops in zone 9 from September transplants have the best flavor and texture. In zone 9 summer heat causes rapid bolting and fibrous, pithy stems; stick to the cool seasons. Harvest when stems are 2–3 inches across for best tenderness.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Grand Duke is the 1979 All-America Selections-winning kohlrabi cultivar and remains the standard cool-season kohlrabi for home gardens. The pale green, smooth-skinned swollen stem enlarges more uniformly and remains tender longer than older varieties — it can be harvested at 2–3 inches and still be mild and crisp, even as other varieties turn woody and pungent. The flavor is mild, sweet, and slightly turnip-like — excellent raw, sliced thin in salads, or stir-fried. In zone 9 kohlrabi is a fall-through-winter vegetable, transplanted in September–October for harvest from November through February. The plants are relatively frost-hardy and will survive most zone 9 winters without protection. Pull the entire plant at harvest; the leaves are also edible when young and cooked like kale.
sources: All-America Selections — Grand Duke Kohlrabi Award Record
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Kohlrabi
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Brassica Crop Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-prepared soil; in zone 9 plant February–March for early summer harvest and June–July for a fall crop. Despite being bred for short northern seasons, Blacktail Mountain performs well in zone 9's heat — the early maturity allows harvest before the most intense late-summer heat, and the dark rind absorbs heat efficiently in cooler spring soil. Check for ripeness by thumping (hollow sound) and examining the tendril nearest the fruit, which should be dried and brown.

directions: Direct sow in hills or rows in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Blacktail Mountain is an early-maturing open-pollinated watermelon developed by Glenn Drowns of Seed Savers Exchange to produce reliably sweet, ripe melons in the short summers of northern Idaho and the northern Plains. The small to medium round fruits (8–12 pounds) have a strikingly dark — almost black-green — rind that distinguishes them instantly from most other watermelon varieties. The bright red, sweet, juicy flesh is comparable to the best commercial watermelons in flavor, with a thin rind and few seeds. In zone 9 the early maturity (70–80 days from direct sow) means it finishes before the most brutal August heat and is particularly useful for succession-planted summer crops that bridge the gap between spring and fall production. The dark rind warms faster in cool spring soil, giving Blacktail Mountain an advantage early in the season when soil temperatures are marginal.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Blacktail Mountain Watermelon
University of Idaho Extension — Watermelon Variety Trial
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Short-Season Watermelons

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant February–March for a June harvest and August for fall. Honey Bear's compact vine habit makes it suitable for smaller garden spaces than standard sprawling acorn squash vines. The small, single-serving fruit size means home gardeners can harvest as needed without worrying about using a large squash before it spoils.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Honey Bear is a 2009 All-America Selections-winning acorn squash that produces a compact, semi-bush vine with small, single-serving fruits — typically 1 pound, the perfect size for splitting and roasting as one serving for two. The deep green, ribbed, acorn-shaped fruits have exceptionally sweet, dry, nutty flesh — rated significantly sweeter in taste trials than standard acorn squash types including Table King and Table Queen. The compact plant habit (3–4 feet spread versus 8–12 feet for standard acorn squash) makes Honey Bear one of the few winter squashes genuinely suitable for raised beds and smaller gardens. Each plant produces multiple fruits over the harvest season. Cure harvested fruits at room temperature for 10 days to convert starches to sugar and extend storage life to 3–4 months.
sources: All-America Selections — Honey Bear Acorn Squash Award Record
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Compact Acorn Squash
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Production
